(sometimes shortened to **gamedev**) is the process of creating a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"). It is a multidisciplinary practice, involving programming, design, art, audio, user interface, and writing. Each of those may be made up of more specialized skills; art includes 3D modeling of objects, character modeling, animation, visual effects, and so on. Development is supported by project management, production, and quality assurance. Teams can be many hundreds of people, a small group, or even a single person.

**Video game development** (sometimes shortened to **gamedev**) is the process of creating a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"). It is a multidisciplinary practice, involving programming, design, art, audio, user interface, and writing. Each of those may be made up of more specialized skills; art includes 3D modeling of objects, character modeling, animation, visual effects, and so on. Development is supported by project management, production, and quality assurance. Teams can be many hundreds of people, a small group, or even a single person.

Development of commercial video games is normally funded by a [publisher](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher") and can take two to five years to reach completion. Game creation by small, self-funded teams is called [independent development](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game"). The technology in a game may be written from scratch or use proprietary software specific to one company. As development has become more complex, it has become common for companies and independent developers alike to use off-the-shelf "engines" such as [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(game_engine) "Unity (game engine)"), [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine") or [Godot](/wiki/Godot_(game_engine) "Godot (game engine)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Commercial [game development](/wiki/Game_development "Game development") began in the 1970s with the advent of [arcade video games](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game"), [first-generation video game consoles](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles") like the [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600"), and [home computers](/wiki/Home_computer "Home computer") like the [Apple II](/wiki/Apple_II "Apple II"). Into the 1980s, a lone [programmer](/wiki/Game_programmer "Game programmer") could develop a full and complete game such as *[Pitfall!](/wiki/Pitfall! "Pitfall!")*. By the second and third generation of video game consoles in the late 1980s, the growing popularity of 3D graphics on personal computers, and higher expectations for visuals and quality, it became difficult for a single person to produce a mainstream video game. The average cost of producing a high-end (often called [AAA](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)")) game slowly rose from US$1–4 million in 2000, to over $200 million and up by 2023. At the same time, independent game development has flourished. The best-selling video game of all time, *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*, was initially written by one person, then supported by a small team, before the company was acquired by [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") and greatly expanded.

Mainstream commercial video games are generally developed in phases. A concept is developed which then moves to [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production") where [prototypes](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype") are written and the plan for the entire game is created. This is followed by full-scale development or *production*, then sometimes a post-production period where the game is polished. It has become common for many developers, especially smaller developers, to publicly release games in an "early access" form, where iterative development takes place in tandem with feedback from actual players.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Games are produced through the [software development](/wiki/Software_development "Software development") process.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke20034-3) They are created both as a form of artistic expression[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke20037-4) and a commercial product intended to generate profit.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200314-5) Game development is often described as a blend of art and science,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200312-6) reflecting the technical precision and creative design required.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Development is normally funded by a [publisher](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"),[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004239-8) and careful budget estimation is critical to success.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Poor planning may cause projects to exceed budgets or fail to meet expectations.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200317-10) In fact, the majority of commercial games do not produce profit.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke20033-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Forbes-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200317–18-13) Most developers therefore plan their production schedule carefully to balance creative goals with available resources.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200318-14)

The game industry requires innovations, as publishers cannot profit from the constant release of repetitive sequels and imitations.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201019-15)[*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Video_game_development "Talk:Video game development")*] Every year new [independent development](/wiki/Independent_game_development "Independent game development") companies open and some manage to develop hit titles. Similarly, many developers close down because they cannot find a publishing contract or their production is not profitable.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201017-16) It is difficult to start a new company due to the high initial investment required.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201037-17) Nevertheless, the growth of the casual and mobile game market has allowed developers with smaller teams to enter the market. Once the companies become financially stable, they may expand to develop larger games.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201017-16) Most developers start small and gradually expand their business.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201037-17) A developer receiving profit from a successful title may store up capital to expand and re-factor their company, as well as tolerate more failed deadlines.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201018-18)

An average development budget for a multiplatform game is [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")18-28M, with high-profile games often exceeding $40M.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the early era of [home computers](/wiki/Home_computer "Home computer") and video game consoles in the early 1980s, a single [programmer](/wiki/Game_programmer "Game programmer") could handle almost all the tasks of developing a game — programming, graphical design, sound effects, etc.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdamsRollings200613-20)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009xxi-21)[[22]](#cite_note-CPGD-22) It could take as little as six weeks to develop a game.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009xxi-21) However, the high user expectations and requirements[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009xxi-21) of modern commercial games far exceed the capabilities of a single developer and require the splitting of responsibilities.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20105-23) A team of over a hundred people can be employed full-time for a single project.[[22]](#cite_note-CPGD-22)

Game development, production, or design is a process that starts from an idea or concept.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates20043-24)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdamsRollings200629–30-25)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200375-26)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20093-27) Often the idea is based on a modification of an existing game concept.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates20043-24)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdamsRollings200631–33-28) The game idea may fall within one or several [genres](/wiki/Game_genre "Game genre").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates20046-29) Designers often experiment with different combinations of genres.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates20046-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland200425-30) A game designer generally writes an initial game proposal document, that describes the basic concept, gameplay, feature list, setting and story, target audience, requirements and schedule, and finally staff and budget estimates.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates200414–16-31) Different companies have different formal procedures and philosophies regarding game design and development.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004151-32)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004151-32)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200923-33) There is no standardized development method; however commonalities exist.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200923-33)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009xxi-xxii-34)

A [game developer](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer") may range from a single individual to a large multinational company. There are both independent and publisher-owned studios.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200925-35) Independent developers rely on financial support from a [game publisher](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200982-36) They usually have to develop a game from concept to prototype without external funding. The formal game proposal is then submitted to publishers, who may finance the game development from several months to years. The publisher would retain exclusive rights to distribute and market the game and would often own the intellectual property rights for the game franchise.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200925-35) The publisher may also own the development studio,[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200925-35)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200987-37) or it may have internal development studio(s). Generally the publisher is the one who owns the game's [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") rights.[[12]](#cite_note-Forbes-12)

Many larger development companies work on several titles at once. This is necessary because of the time taken between shipping a game and receiving royalty payments, which may be between 6 and 18 months. Small companies may structure contracts, ask for advances on royalties, use shareware distribution, employ part-time workers and use other methods to meet payroll demands.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200926-38)

[Console manufacturers](/wiki/Console_manufacturer "Console manufacturer"), such as [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), or [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), have a standard set of technical requirements that a game must conform to in order to be approved. Additionally, the game concept must be approved by the manufacturer, who may refuse to approve certain titles.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200990-39)

Most modern PC or console games take from three to five years to complete[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], whereas a mobile game can be developed in a few months.[[40]](#cite_note-MGA_SoA-40) The length of development is influenced by a number of factors, such as [genre](/wiki/Computer_and_video_game_genres "Computer and video game genres"), scale, development platform and number of assets.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some games can take much longer than the average time frame to complete. An infamous example is [3D Realms](/wiki/3D_Realms "3D Realms")' *[Duke Nukem Forever](/wiki/Duke_Nukem_Forever "Duke Nukem Forever")*, announced to be in production in April 1997 and released fourteen years later in June 2011.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Planning for Maxis' game *[Spore](/wiki/Spore_(2008_video_game) "Spore (2008 video game)")* began in late 1999; the game was released nine years later in September 2008.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The game *[Prey](/wiki/Prey_(2006_video_game) "Prey (2006 video game)")* was briefly profiled in a 1997 issue of PC Gamer, but was not released until 2006, and only then in highly altered form. Finally, *[Team Fortress 2](/wiki/Team_Fortress_2 "Team Fortress 2")* was in development from 1998 until its 2007 release, and emerged from a convoluted development process involving "probably three or four different games", according to [Gabe Newell](/wiki/Gabe_Newell "Gabe Newell").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The game revenue from retail is divided among the parties along the distribution chain, such as — developer, publisher, retail, manufacturer and console royalty. Many developers fail to profit from this and go bankrupt.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200926-38) Many seek alternative economic models through Internet marketing and distribution channels to improve returns,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200926–27-43) as through a mobile distribution channel the share of a developer can be up to 70% of the total revenue[[40]](#cite_note-MGA_SoA-40) and through an online distribution channel owned by the developer almost 100%.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/VideoGamesSystemHistoryMural.jpg/960px-VideoGamesSystemHistoryMural.jpg)](/wiki/File:VideoGamesSystemHistoryMural.jpg)

Video games system history timeline

The history of game making begins with the development of the [first video games](/wiki/First_video_game "First video game"), although which video game is the first depends on the definition of *video game*. The first games created had little entertainment value, and their development focus was separate from user experience—in fact, these games required mainframe computers to play them.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20107-44) *[OXO](/wiki/OXO_(video_game) "OXO (video game)")*, written by [Alexander S. Douglas](/wiki/Alexander_S._Douglas "Alexander S. Douglas") in 1952, was the first computer game to use a digital display.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20105-23) In 1958, a game called *[Tennis for Two](/wiki/Tennis_for_Two "Tennis for Two")*, which displayed its output on an oscilloscope, was made by [Willy Higinbotham](/wiki/Willy_Higinbotham "Willy Higinbotham"), a physicist working at the [Brookhaven National Laboratory](/wiki/Brookhaven_National_Laboratory "Brookhaven National Laboratory").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20106-45)[[46]](#cite_note-1983magazine-46) In 1961, a mainframe computer game called *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* was developed by a group of [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") students led by [Steve Russell](/wiki/Steve_Russell_(computer_scientist) "Steve Russell (computer scientist)").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20106-45)

True commercial design and development of games began in the 1970s, when [arcade video games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") and [first-generation consoles](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles") were marketed. In 1971, *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")* was the first commercially sold, coin-operated video game. It used a black-and-white [television](/wiki/Television "Television") for its display, and the computer system was made of [74 series](/wiki/7400_series "7400 series") [TTL](/wiki/Transistor-transistor_logic "Transistor-transistor logic") [chips](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit").[[47]](#cite_note-47) In 1972, the first home console system was released called [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey"), developed by [Ralph H. Baer](/wiki/Ralph_H._Baer "Ralph H. Baer").[[48]](#cite_note-48) That same year, [Atari](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc.") released *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, an arcade game that increased video game popularity.[[49]](#cite_note-PBS-GameRev-Time-49) The commercial success of *Pong* led other companies to develop *Pong* clones, spawning the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry").[[50]](#cite_note-InformIT-50)

Programmers worked within the big companies to produce games for these devices. The industry did not see huge innovation in game design and a large number of consoles had very similar games.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20109-51) Many of these early games were often *Pong* clones.[[52]](#cite_note-Whittaker-122-52) Some games were different, however, such as *[Gun Fight](/wiki/Gun_Fight "Gun Fight")*, which was significant for several reasons:[[53]](#cite_note-allgame-53) an early 1975 [on-foot](/wiki/Run_and_gun_(video_game) "Run and gun (video game)"), [multi-directional shooter](/wiki/Multi-directional_shooter "Multi-directional shooter"),[[54]](#cite_note-kotaku_gun-54) which depicted [game characters](/wiki/Player_character "Player character"),[[55]](#cite_note-kohler19-55) [game violence](/wiki/Video_game_violence "Video game violence"), and human-to-human [combat](/wiki/Action_game "Action game").[[56]](#cite_note-Steinberg-56) [Tomohiro Nishikado](/wiki/Tomohiro_Nishikado "Tomohiro Nishikado")'s original version was based on [discrete logic](/wiki/Discrete_logic "Discrete logic"),[[57]](#cite_note-kohler18-57) which [Dave Nutting](/wiki/Dave_Nutting "Dave Nutting") adapted using the [Intel 8080](/wiki/Intel_8080 "Intel 8080"), making it the first video game to use a [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor").[[58]](#cite_note-Kent-58) Console manufacturers soon started to produce consoles that were able to play independently developed games,[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201013-59) and ran on microprocessors, marking the beginning of [second-generation consoles](/wiki/Second_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Second generation of video game consoles"), beginning with the release of the [Fairchild Channel F](/wiki/Fairchild_Channel_F "Fairchild Channel F") in 1976.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The flood of *Pong* clones led to the [video game crash of 1977](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1977 "Video game crash of 1977"), which eventually came to an end with the mainstream success of [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito")'s 1978 arcade [shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*,[[52]](#cite_note-Whittaker-122-52) marking the beginning of the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") and inspiring dozens of manufacturers to enter the market.[[52]](#cite_note-Whittaker-122-52)[[60]](#cite_note-Kent-500-60) Its creator Nishikado not only designed and programmed the game, but also did the artwork, engineered the [arcade hardware](/wiki/Video_game_arcade_cabinet "Video game arcade cabinet"), and put together a [microcomputer](/wiki/Microcomputer "Microcomputer") from scratch.[[61]](#cite_note-1UP-10things-61) It was soon [ported](/wiki/Porting "Porting") to the [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600"), becoming the first "[killer app](/wiki/Killer_app "Killer app")" and quadrupling the console's sales.[[62]](#cite_note-RG-41-62) At the same time, [home computers](/wiki/Home_computer "Home computer") appeared on the market, allowing individual programmers and hobbyists to develop games. This allowed hardware manufacturer and software manufacturers to act separately. A very large number of games could be produced by an individual, as games were easy to make because graphical and memory limitation did not allow for much content. Larger companies developed, who focused selected teams to work on a title.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201012-63) The developers of many early home video games, such as *[Zork](/wiki/Zork "Zork")*, *[Baseball](/wiki/Baseball_(computer_game) "Baseball (computer game)")*, *[Air Warrior](/wiki/Air_Warrior_(video_game) "Air Warrior (video game)")*, and *[Adventure](/wiki/Colossal_Cave_Adventure "Colossal Cave Adventure")*, later transitioned their work as products of the early video game industry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The industry expanded significantly at the time, with the arcade video game sector alone (representing the largest share of the gaming industry) generating higher revenues than both [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") and [Hollywood films](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") combined.[[64]](#cite_note-Rogers-Larsen-263-64) The home video game industry, however, suffered major losses following the [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983").[[65]](#cite_note-65) In 1984 [Jon Freeman](/wiki/Jon_Freeman_(game_designer) "Jon Freeman (game designer)") warned in *Computer Gaming World*:

> *Q: Are computer games the way to fame and fortune?*
>
> A: No. Not unless your idea of fame is having your name recognized by one or two astute individuals at [Origins](/wiki/Origins_Game_Fair "Origins Game Fair") ... I've been making a living (after a fashion) designing games for most of the last six years. I wouldn't recommend it for someone with a weak heart or a large appetite, though.[[66]](#cite_note-freeman198412-66)

[Chris Crawford](/wiki/Chris_Crawford_(game_designer) "Chris Crawford (game designer)") and [Don Daglow](/wiki/Don_Daglow "Don Daglow") in 1987 similarly advised prospective designers to write games as a hobby first, and to not quit their existing jobs early.[[67]](#cite_note-cgw198702-67)[[68]](#cite_note-daglow198709-68) The home video game industry was revitalized soon after by the widespread success of the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

*[Compute!'s Gazette](/wiki/Compute!%27s_Gazette "Compute!'s Gazette")* in 1986 stated that although individuals developed most early video games, "It's impossible for one person to have the multiple talents necessary to create a good game".[[70]](#cite_note-Yakal198606-70) By 1987 a video game required 12 months to develop and another six to plan marketing. Projects remained usually solo efforts, with single developers delivering finished games to their publishers.[[68]](#cite_note-daglow198709-68) With the ever-increasing processing and graphical capabilities of arcade, console, and computer products, along with an increase in user expectations, game design moved beyond the scope of a single developer to produce a marketable game.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The *Gazette* stated, "The process of writing a game involves coming up with an original, entertaining concept, having the skill to bring it to fruition through good, efficient programming, and also being a fairly respectable artist".[[70]](#cite_note-Yakal198606-70) This sparked the beginning of team-based development.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In broad terms, during the 1980s, pre-production involved sketches and test routines of the only developer. In the 1990s, pre-production consisted mostly of game art previews. In the early 2000s, pre-production usually produced a [playable demo](/wiki/First_playable_demo "First playable demo").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200326-72)

In 2000 a 12 to 36 month development project was funded by a publisher for [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1M–3M.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200315-73) Additionally, $250k–1.5M were spent on marketing and sales development.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200315–16-74) In 2001, over 3000 games were released for PC; and from about 100 games turning profit only about 50 made significant profit.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200315-73) In the early 2000s it became increasingly common to use [middleware game engines](/wiki/Game_Middleware "Game Middleware"), such as [*Quake* engine](/wiki/Quake_engine "Quake engine") or [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200330-75)

In the early 2000s, also [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_games "Mobile games") started to gain popularity. However, mobile games distributed by mobile operators remained a marginal form of gaming until the Apple [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)") was launched in 2008.[[40]](#cite_note-MGA_SoA-40)

In 2005, a mainstream console video game cost from [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")3M to $6M to develop. Some games cost as much as $20M to develop.[[76]](#cite_note-76) In 2006 the profit from a console game sold at retail was divided among parties of distribution chain as follows: developer (13%), publisher (32%), retail (32%), manufacturer (5%), console royalty (18%).[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200926-38) In 2008 a developer would retain around 17% of retail price and around 85% if sold online.[[12]](#cite_note-Forbes-12)

Since the [third generation of consoles](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles"), the home video game industry has constantly increased and expanded. The industry revenue has increased at least five-fold since the 1990s. In 2007, the software portion of video game revenue was $9.5 billion, exceeding that of the movie industry.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201015-77)

The Apple App Store, introduced in 2008, was the first mobile application store operated directly by the [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system")-[platform holder](/wiki/Platform_holder "Platform holder"). It significantly changed the [consumer behaviour](/wiki/Consumer_behaviour "Consumer behaviour") more favourable for downloading mobile content and quickly broadened the markets of mobile games.[[40]](#cite_note-MGA_SoA-40)

In 2009 games' market annual value was estimated between $7–30 billion, depending on which sales figures are included. This is on par with films' box office market.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200924-78) A publisher would typically fund an independent developer for $500k–$5M for a development of a title.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200925-35) In 2012, the total value had already reached $66.3 billion and by then the video game markets were no longer dominated by console games. According to Newzoo, the share of MMO's was 19.8%, PC/MAC's 9.8%, tablets' 3.2%, smartphones 10.6%, handhelds' 9.8%, consoles' only 36.7% and online casual games 10.2%. The fastest growing market segments being mobile games with an average annual rate of 19% for smartphones and 48% for tablets.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In the past several years, many developers opened and many closed down. Each year a number of developers are acquired by larger companies or merge with existing companies. For example, in 2007 [Blizzard Entertainment](/wiki/Blizzard_Entertainment "Blizzard Entertainment")'s parent company, [Vivendi Games](/wiki/Vivendi_Games "Vivendi Games") merged with [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision"). In 2008 [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") nearly acquired [Take-Two Interactive](/wiki/Take-Two_Interactive "Take-Two Interactive"). In 2009 [Midway Games](/wiki/Midway_Games "Midway Games") was acquired by [Time-Warner](/wiki/Time-Warner "Time-Warner") and [Eidos Interactive](/wiki/Eidos_Interactive "Eidos Interactive") merged with [Square Enix](/wiki/Square_Enix "Square Enix").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201016-80)

## Roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roles")]

### Producer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Producer")]

Main article: [Video game producer](/wiki/Video_game_producer "Video game producer")

Development is overseen by internal and external producers.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004154-81)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201071-82) The producer working for the developer is known as the *internal producer* and manages the development team, schedules, reports progress, hires and assigns staff, and so on.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201071-82)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004156–158-83) The producer working for the publisher is known as the *external producer* and oversees developer progress and budget.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004154–156-84) Producer's responsibilities include [PR](/wiki/Public_relations "Public relations"), contract negotiation, liaising between the staff and stakeholders, schedule and budget maintenance, [quality assurance](/wiki/Quality_assurance "Quality assurance"), [beta](/wiki/Betaware "Betaware") test management, and localization.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201071-82)[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004153-85) This role may also be referred to as *project manager*, *project lead*, or *director*.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201071-82)[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004153-85)

### Publisher

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Publisher")]

Main article: [Video game publisher](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher")
