A video game publisher is a company that publishes video games that they have either developed internally or have had developed by an external video game developer. As with book publishers or publishers of DVD movies, video game publishers are responsible for their product's manufacturing and marketing, including market research and all aspects of advertising.

They usually finance the development, sometimes by paying a video game developer (the publisher calls this external development) and sometimes by paying an internal staff of developers called a studio. Consequently, they also typically own the IP of the game.[[40]](#cite_note-MGA_SoA-40) Large video game publishers also distribute the games they publish, while some smaller publishers instead hire distribution companies (or larger video game publishers) to distribute the games they publish.

Other functions usually performed by the publisher include deciding on and paying for any license that the game may utilize; paying for localization; layout, printing, and possibly the writing of the user manual; and the creation of graphic design elements such as the box design.

Large publishers may also attempt to boost efficiency across all internal and external development teams by providing services such as sound design and code packages for commonly needed functionality.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Because the publisher usually finances development, it usually tries to manage development risk with a staff of producers or project managers to monitor the progress of the developer, critique ongoing development, and assist as necessary. Most video games created by an external video game developer are paid for with periodic advances on royalties. These advances are paid when the developer reaches certain stages of development, called milestones.

[Independent video game developers](/wiki/Independent_video_game_development "Independent video game development") create games without a publisher and may choose to digitally distribute their games.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Development team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Development team")]

Developers can range in size from small groups making casual games to housing hundreds of employees and producing several large titles.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201037-17) Companies divide their subtasks of game development. Individual job titles may vary; however, roles are the same within the industry.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004151-32) The development team consists of several members.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak20105-23) Some members of the team may handle more than one role; similarly, more than one task may be handled by the same member.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004151-32) Team size can vary from 3 to 100 or more members, depending on the game's scope. The most represented are artists, followed by programmers, then designers, and finally, audio specialists, with one to three producers in management.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201025-87) Many teams also include a dedicated writer with expertise in [video game writing](/wiki/Video_game_writing "Video game writing").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These positions are employed full-time. Other positions, such as testers, may be employed only part-time.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201025-87) Use of contractors for art, programming, and writing is standard within the industry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Salaries for these positions vary depending on both the experience and the location of the employee.[[88]](#cite_note-BigFish-88)

A development team includes these roles or disciplines:[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004151-32)

#### Designer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Designer")]

Further information: [Video game design](/wiki/Video_game_design "Video game design")

A game designer is a person who designs [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay"), conceiving and designing the rules and structure of a [game](/wiki/Game "Game").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalenZimmerman2003-89)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland2004292-90)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201074-91) Development teams usually have a lead designer who coordinates the work of other designers. They are the main visionaries of the game.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland2004292–296-92) One of the roles of a designer is being a writer, often employed part-time to conceive the game's [narrative](/wiki/Narrative "Narrative"), dialogue, commentary, [cutscene](/wiki/Cutscene "Cutscene") narrative, journals, [video game packaging](/wiki/Video_game_packaging "Video game packaging") content, hint system, etc.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004163-93)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrathwaiteSchreiber2009171-94)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201094-95) In larger projects, there are often separate designers for various parts of the game, such as, [game mechanics](/wiki/Game_mechanics "Game mechanics"), [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface"), [characters](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)"), [dialogue](/wiki/Dialogue "Dialogue"), [graphics](/wiki/Graphics "Graphics"), etc.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Artist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Artist")]

Further information: [Game art design](/wiki/Game_art_design "Game art design")

A game artist is a [visual artist](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts") who creates video game art.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004171-96)[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201085-97) The art production is usually overseen by an *art director* or *art lead*, making sure their vision is followed. The art director manages the art team, scheduling and coordinating within the development team.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004171-96)

The artist's job may be [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") oriented or [3D](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") oriented. *2D artists* may produce concept art,[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201086-98)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004173-99) sprites,[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201087-100) [textures](/wiki/Texture_(visual_arts) "Texture (visual arts)"),[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201088-101)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004176-102) environmental backdrops or terrain images,[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201086-98)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004176-102) and user interface.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201087-100) *3D artists* may produce models or [meshes](/wiki/Polygon_mesh "Polygon mesh"),[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201089-103)[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004175-104) animation,[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201089-103) 3D environment,[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201090-105) and cinematics.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201090-105) Artists sometimes occupy both roles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Programmer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Programmer")]

Main article: [Game programmer](/wiki/Game_programmer "Game programmer")

A game programmer is a [software engineer](/wiki/Software_engineer "Software engineer") who primarily develops video games or related software (such as [game development tools](/wiki/Game_development_tool "Game development tool")). The game's [codebase](/wiki/Codebase "Codebase") development is handled by programmers.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004168-106)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201078-107) There are usually one to several [lead programmers](/wiki/Lead_programmer "Lead programmer"),[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004165-108) who implement the game's starting codebase and overview future development and programmer allocation on individual modules. An entry-level programmer can make, on average, around $70,000 annually and an experienced programmer can make, on average, around $125,000 annually.[[88]](#cite_note-BigFish-88)

Individual programming disciplines roles include:[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004168-106)

* [Physics](/wiki/Game_physics "Game physics") – the programming of the [game engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine"), including simulating physics, [collision](/wiki/Collision_detection "Collision detection"), object movement, etc.;
* [AI](/wiki/Game_artificial_intelligence "Game artificial intelligence") – producing computer agents using game AI techniques, such as scripting, planning, rule-based decisions, etc.
* Graphics – the managing of graphical content utilization and memory considerations; the production of the graphics engine, integration of [models](/wiki/3D_modeling "3D modeling"), textures to work along the [physics engine](/wiki/Physics_engine "Physics engine").
* [Sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") – integration of music, speech, and effect sounds into the proper locations and times.
* [Gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay") – implementation of various game rules and features (sometimes called a *generalist*);
* Scripting – development and maintenance of a high-level command system for various in-game tasks, such as AI, level editor triggers, etc.
* [UI](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") – production of user interface elements, like option menus, [HUDs](/wiki/HUD_(video_gaming) "HUD (video gaming)"), help and feedback systems, etc.
* Input processing – processing and compatibility correlation of various input devices, such as keyboard, mouse, gamepad, etc.
* Network communications – the managing of data inputs and outputs for local and internet gameplay.
* [Game tools](/wiki/Game_development_tool "Game development tool") – the production of tools to accompany the development of the game, especially for designers and scripters.

#### Level designer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Level designer")]

Further information: [Level design](/wiki/Level_design "Level design")

A level designer is a person who creates [levels](/wiki/Level_(computer_and_video_games) "Level (computer and video games)"), challenges or missions for video games using a specific set of [programs](/wiki/Computer_program "Computer program").[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004162-109)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201076-110) These programs may be commonly available commercial [3D](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") or [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") design programs, or specially designed and tailored [level editors](/wiki/Level_editor "Level editor") made for a specific game.

Level designers work with both incomplete and complete versions of the game. Game programmers usually produce level editors and design tools for the designers to use. This eliminates the need for designers to access or modify game code.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Level editors may involve custom [high-level](/wiki/High-level_programming_language "High-level programming language") [scripting languages](/wiki/Scripting_language "Scripting language") for interactive environments or AIs. As opposed to the level editing tools sometimes available to the community, level designers often work with placeholders and prototypes aiming for consistency and clear layout before the required artwork is completed.

#### Sound engineer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sound engineer")]

Further information: [Video game music](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music")

Sound engineers are technical professionals responsible for [sound effects](/wiki/Sound_effect "Sound effect") and sound positioning. They are sometimes involved in creating haptic feedback, as was the case with the [Returnal](/wiki/Returnal_(video_game) "Returnal (video game)") game sound team at PlayStation Studios Creative Arts in London.[[112]](#cite_note-112)
They sometimes oversee [voice acting](/wiki/Voice_acting "Voice acting") and other sound asset creation.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004185,_188,_191-113)[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201091-114) [Composers](/wiki/Composer "Composer") who create a game's [musical score](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music") also comprise a game's sound team, though often this work is outsourced.

#### Tester

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tester")]

Further information: [Game testing](/wiki/Game_testing "Game testing")

The [quality assurance](/wiki/Quality_assurance "Quality assurance") is carried out by game testers. A game tester analyzes [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") to document [software defects](/wiki/Software_bug "Software bug") as part of a [quality control](/wiki/Quality_control "Quality control"). Testing is a highly technical field requiring [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing") expertise and analytic competence.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004176-102)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201095-115)

The testers ensure that the game falls within the proposed design: it both works and is entertaining.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004177-116) This involves testing of all features, compatibility, [localization](/wiki/Game_localization "Game localization"), etc. Although necessary throughout the whole development process, testing is expensive and is often actively utilized only towards the completion of the project.

## Development process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Development process")]

Game development is a software development process, as a video game is software with art, audio, and gameplay. Formal software development methods are often overlooked.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke20034-3) Games with poor development methodology are likely to run over budget and time estimates, as well as contain a large number of bugs. Planning is important for individual[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke20033-11) and group projects alike.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200315-73)

Overall game development is not suited for typical [software life cycle](/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle "Software release life cycle") methods, such as the [waterfall model](/wiki/Waterfall_model "Waterfall model").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004225-117)

One method employed for game development is [agile development](/wiki/Agile_software_development "Agile software development").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004218–219-118) It is based on [iterative prototyping](/wiki/Iterative_design "Iterative design"), a subset of [software prototyping](/wiki/Software_prototyping "Software prototyping").[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004226–227-119) Agile development depends on feedback and refinement of the game's iterations with a gradually increasing feature set.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200947-120) This method is effective because most projects do not start with a clear requirement outline.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004218–219-118) A popular method of agile software development is [Scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(development) "Scrum (development)").[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200941-121)

Another successful method is [Personal Software Process](/wiki/Personal_Software_Process "Personal Software Process") (PSP) requiring additional staff training to increase awareness of project planning.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200941,_43–44-122) This method is more expensive and requires commitment of team members. PSP can be extended to [Team Software Process](/wiki/Team_Software_Process "Team Software Process"), where the whole team is self-directing.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler200944-123)

Game development usually involves an overlap of these methods.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004225-117) For example, asset creation may be done via waterfall model, because requirements and specification are clear,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004227-124) but gameplay design might be done using iterative prototyping.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004227-124)

There are many types of commonly used programming languages for game development as more suitable. Java and Javascript are top choices due to animation capability. Many 3D games also use C++ or C#. Python also has many libraries that can produce suitable game development. Minecraft for example is built entirely through Java. When programming the game the developers can either program
the game animation using many graphical design libraries within the programming language. Or if not they can load images direct into memory using the code to be the game animation. Java has a high functionality for loading images into memory. Development of a commercial game usually includes the following stages:[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004203-126)

### Pre-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pre-production")]

*Pre-production*[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127) or *design phase*[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200326-72) is a planning phase of the project focused on idea and concept development and production of initial design documents.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004203-126)[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdamsRollings200629-128)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland2004251-129)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20095–9-130) The goal of concept development is to produce clear and easy to understand documentation,[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004203-126)[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20096-131) which describes all the tasks, schedules and estimates for the development team.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003102-132) The suite of documents produced in this phase is called [production plan](/wiki/Production_plan "Production plan").[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003101–102-133) This phase is usually not funded by a publisher,[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004203-126) however good publishers may require developers to produce plans during pre-production.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003102-132)

The concept documentation can be separated into three stages or documents—high concept, pitch and concept;[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004203–207-134) however, there is no industry standard naming convention, for example, both Bethke (2003) and Bates (2004) refer to *pitch document* as "game proposal",[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003102-132) yet Moore, Novak (2010) refers to *concept document* as "game proposal".[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)

The late stage of pre-production may also be referred to as *proof of concept*,[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127) or *technical review*[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125) when more detailed game documents are produced.

Publishers have started to expect broader game proposals even featuring playable prototypes.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003103-135)

#### High concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: High concept")]

*High concept* is a brief description of a game.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127) The high concept is the one- or two-sentence response to the question, "What is your game about?".

#### Pitch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pitch")]

A *pitch*,[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127) *concept document*,[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125) proposal document,[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003102-132) or *game proposal*[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127) is a short summary document intended to present the game's selling points and detail why the game would be profitable to develop.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127)

Verbal pitches may be made to management within the developer company, and then presented to publishers.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004274-136) A written document may need to be shown to publishers before funding is approved.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003102-132) A game proposal may undergo one to several *green-light meetings* with publisher executives who determine if the game is to be developed.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200327-137) The presentation of the project is often given by the game designers.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) [Demos](/wiki/Game_demo "Game demo") may be created for the pitch; however may be unnecessary for established developers with good track records.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138)

If the developer acts as its own publisher, or both companies are subsidiaries of a single company, then only the upper management needs to give approval.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138)

#### Concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Concept")]

*Concept document*,[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127) *game proposal*,[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125) or *game plan*[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20098-139) is a more detailed document than the pitch document.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201070-125)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204-127)[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20096-131) This includes all the information produced about the game.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20098-139) This includes the high concept, game's genre, gameplay description, features, setting, story, target audience, hardware platforms, estimated schedule, marketing analysis, team requirements, and risk analysis.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004204–205-140)

Before an approved design is completed, a skeleton crew of programmers and artists usually begins work.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) Programmers may develop quick-and-dirty prototypes showcasing one or more features that stakeholders would like to see incorporated in the final product.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) Artists may develop concept art and asset sketches as a springboard for developing real [game assets](/wiki/Game_asset "Game asset").[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) Producers may work part-time on the game at this point, scaling up for full-time commitment as development progresses.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) Game producer's work during pre-production is related to planning the schedule, budget and estimating tasks with the team.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) The producer aims to create a solid production plan so that no delays are experienced at the start of the production.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138)

#### Game design document

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Game design document")]

Main article: [Game design document](/wiki/Game_design_document "Game design document")

Before a full-scale production can begin, the development team produces the first version of a [game design document](/wiki/Game_design_document "Game design document") incorporating all or most of the material from the initial pitch.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004276-141)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland2004240,_274-142) The design document describes the game's concept and major gameplay elements in detail. It may also include preliminary sketches of various aspects of the game. The design document is sometimes accompanied by functional [prototypes](/wiki/Software_prototyping "Software prototyping") of some sections of the game.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The design document remains a [living document](/wiki/Living_document "Living document") throughout the development—often changed weekly or even daily.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland2004241-143)

Compiling a list of game's needs is called "requirement capture".[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke20033-11)

#### Prototype

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Prototype")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Battle_for_Mandicor_0.0.5.png/250px-Battle_for_Mandicor_0.0.5.png)](/wiki/File:Battle_for_Mandicor_0.0.5.png)

Placeholder graphics are characteristic of early game prototypes.

Writing [prototypes](/wiki/Software_prototyping "Software prototyping") of gameplay ideas and features is an important activity that allows programmers and game designers to experiment with different [algorithms](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") and usability scenarios for a game. A great deal of prototyping may take place during [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production") before the design document is complete and may, in fact, help determine what features the design specifies. Prototyping at this stage is often done manually, (paper prototyping), not digitally,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] as this is often easier and faster to test and make changes before wasting time and resources on what could be a canceled idea or project. Prototyping may also take place during active development to test new ideas as the game emerges.

Prototypes are often meant only to act as a [proof of concept](/wiki/Proof_of_concept "Proof of concept") or to test ideas, by adding, modifying or removing some of the features.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrathwaiteSchreiber2009189-144) Most algorithms and features debuted in a prototype may be [ported](/wiki/Porting "Porting") to the game once they have been completed.

Often prototypes need to be developed quickly with very little time for up-front design (around 20 to 25§ minutes of testing).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Therefore, usually very prolific programmers are called upon to quickly code these [testbed](/wiki/Testbed "Testbed") tools. [RAD](/wiki/Rapid_application_development "Rapid application development") tools may be used to aid in the quick development of these programs. In case the prototype is in a physical form, programmers and designers alike will make the game with paper, dice, and other easy-to-access tools in order to make the prototype faster.

A successful development model is [iterative prototyping](/wiki/Iterative_design "Iterative design"), where design is refined based on current progress. There are various technology available for video game development[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004226-145)

### Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Production")]

Production is the main stage of development when assets and [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") for the game are produced.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20099-146)

Mainstream production is usually defined as the period of time when the project is fully staffed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Programmers write new [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code"), artists develop [game assets](/wiki/Game_asset "Game asset"), such as, [sprites](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics) "Sprite (computer graphics)") or [3D models](/wiki/3D_model "3D model"). Sound engineers develop sound effects and composers develop music for the game. [Level designers](/wiki/Level_designer "Level designer") create levels, and writers write dialogue for cutscenes and [NPCs](/wiki/Non-player_character "Non-player character").[*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*] Game designers continue to develop the game's design throughout production.

#### Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Design")]

Main article: [Game design](/wiki/Game_design "Game design")

Game design is an essential and collaborative[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004xxi-147) process of designing the content and rules of a [game](/wiki/Game "Game"),[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrathwaiteSchreiber20092-148) requiring artistic and technical competence as well as writing skills.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdamsRollings200620,_22–23,_24–25-149) Creativity and an open mind are vital for the completion of a successful video game.

During development, the game designer implements and modifies the game design to reflect the current vision of the game. Features and levels are often removed or added. The art treatment may evolve and the backstory may change. A new [platform](/wiki/Computing_platform "Computing platform") may be targeted as well as a new [demographic](/wiki/Demographic "Demographic"). All these changes need to be documented and disseminated to the rest of the team. Most changes occur as updates to the design document.

#### Programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Programming")]

Main article: [Game programming](/wiki/Game_programming "Game programming")

The programming of the game is handled by one or more [game programmers](/wiki/Game_programmer "Game programmer"). They develop prototypes to test ideas, many of which may never make it into the final game. The programmers incorporate new features demanded by the game design and fix any bugs introduced during the development process. Even if an off-the-shelf [game engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine") is used, a great deal of programming is required to customize almost every game.

#### Level creation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Level creation")]

Main article: [Level design](/wiki/Level_design "Level design")

From a time standpoint, the game's first level takes the longest to develop. As level designers and artists use the tools for level building, they request features and changes to the in-house tools that allow for quicker and higher-quality development. Newly introduced features may cause old levels to become obsolete, so the levels developed early on may be repeatedly developed and discarded. Because of the dynamic environment of game development, the design of early levels may also change over time. It is not uncommon to spend upwards of twelve months on one level of a game developed over the course of three years. Later levels can be developed much more quickly as the feature set is more complete and the game vision is clearer and more stable.

#### Art production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Art production")]

Main article: [Game art design](/wiki/Game_art_design "Game art design")

During development, artists make art assets according to specifications given by the designers. Early in production, concept artists make concept art to guide the artistic direction of the game, rough art is made for prototypes, and the designers work with artists to design the visual style and visual language of the game. As production goes on, more final art is made, and existing art is edited based on player feedback.

#### Audio production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Audio production")]

Further information: [Video game music](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music")

Game audio may be separated into three categories—sound effects, music, and voice-over.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200349-150)

Sound effect production is the production of sounds by either tweaking a sample to a desired effect or replicating it with real objects.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200349-150) Sound effects include UI sound design, which effectively conveys information both for visible UI elements and as an auditory display. It provides sonic feedback for in-game interfaces, as well as contributing to the overall game aesthetic.[[151]](#cite_note-151) Sound effects are important and impact the game's delivery.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003363-152)

Music may be synthesized or performed live.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200350-153)

There are four main ways in which music is presented in a game.

* Music may be ambient, especially for slow periods of game, where the music aims to reinforce the aesthetic mood and game setting.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003344-154)
* Music may be triggered by in-game events. For example, in such games as [Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man") or [Mario](/wiki/Mario "Mario"), player picking up [power-ups](/wiki/Power-up "Power-up") triggered respective musical scores.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003344-154)
* Action music, such as chase, battle or hunting sequences is fast-paced, hard-changing score.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003345-155)
* Menu music, similar to credits music, creates aural impact while relatively little action is taking place.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003345-155)

A game title with 20 hours of single-player gameplay may feature around 1 hour.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003345-155)

#### Testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Testing")]

Main article: [Game testing](/wiki/Game_testing "Game testing")

[Quality assurance](/wiki/Quality_assurance "Quality assurance") of a video game product plays a significant role throughout the development cycle of a game, though comes more significantly into play as the game nears completion. Unlike other software products or productivity applications, video games are fundamentally meant to entertain, and thus the testing of video games is more focused on the end-user experience rather than the accuracy of the software code's performance, which leads to differences in how the game software is developed.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156)

Because game development is focused on the presentation and gameplay as seen by the player, there often is little rigor in maintaining and testing backend code in the early stages of development since such code may be readily disregarded if there are changes found in gameplay. Some [automated testing](/wiki/Test_automation "Test automation") may be used to ensure the core game engine operates as expected, but most game testing comes via [game tester](/wiki/Game_tester "Game tester"), who enter the testing process once a playable prototype is available. This may be one level or subset of the game software that can be used to any reasonable extent.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156) The use of testers may be lightweight at the early stages of development, but the testers' role becomes more predominant as the game nears completion, becoming a full-time role alongside development.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156) Early testing is considered a key part of game design; the most common issue raised in several published post-mortems on game development was the failure to start the testing process early.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156)

As code matures and the gameplay features solidify, then development typically includes more rigorous test controls such as [regression testing](/wiki/Regression_testing "Regression testing") to make sure new updates to the code base do not change working parts of the game. Games are complex software systems, and changes in one code area may unexpected cause a seemingly unrelated part of the game to fail. Testers are tasked to repeatedly play through updated versions of games in these later stages to look for any issues or bugs not otherwise found from automated testing. Because this can be a monotonous task of playing the same game over and over, this process can lead to games frequently being released with uncaught bugs or glitches.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156)

There are other factors simply inherent to video games that can make testing difficult. This includes the use of randomized gameplay systems, which require more testing for both game balance and bug tracking than more linearized games, the balance of cost and time to devote to testing as part of the development budget, and assuring that the game still remains fun and entertaining to play as changes are made to it.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156)

Despite the dangers of overlooking regression testing, some game developers and publishers fail to test the full feature suite of the game and ship a game with bugs. This can result in customer dissatisfaction and failure to meet sales goals. When this does happen, most developers and publishers quickly release [patches](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)") that fix the bugs and make the game fully playable again.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156) Certain publishing models are designed specifically to accommodate the fact that first releases of games may be bug-ridden but will be fixed post-release. The [early access](/wiki/Early_access "Early access") model invites players to pay into a game before its planned release and helps to provide feedback and bug reports.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156) [Mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_games "Mobile games") and games with live services are also anticipated to be updated on a frequent basis, offset by pre-release testing with live feedback and bug reports.[[156]](#cite_note-gamedev_survey-156)

### Milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Milestones")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Software_dev2.svg/250px-Software_dev2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Software_dev2.svg)

Video game development milestones follow a similar process as with other software development.

Commercial game development projects may be required to meet milestones set by the publisher. Milestones mark major events during game development and are used to track game's progress.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009244-157) Such milestones may be, for example, *first playable*,[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003293-158)[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009244–245-159) *alpha*,[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003294-160)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161) or *beta*[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161) game versions. Project milestones depend on the developer schedules.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009244-157)

Milestones are usually based on multiple short descriptions for functionality; examples may be "Player roaming around in-game environment" or "Physics working, collisions, vehicle" etc. (numerous descriptions are possible). These milestones are usually how the developer gets paid; sometimes as "an advance against royalty". These milestones are listed, anywhere from three to twenty depending on the developer and publisher. The milestone list is usually a collaborative agreement between the publisher and developer. The developer usually advocates for making the milestone descriptions as simple as possible; depending on the specific publisher - the milestone agreements may get very detailed for a specific game. When working with a good publisher, the "spirit of the law" is usually adhered to regarding milestone completion... in other words, if the milestone is 90% complete the milestone is usually paid with the understanding that it will be 100% complete by the next due milestone. It is a collaborative agreement between publisher and developer, and usually (but not always) the developer is constrained by heavy monthly development expenses that need to be met. Also, sometimes milestones are "swapped", the developer or publisher may mutually agree to amend the agreement and rearrange milestone goals depending on changing requirements and development resources available. Milestone agreements are usually included as part of the legal development contracts. After each "milestone" there is usually a payment arrangement. Some very established developers may simply have a milestone agreement based on the amount of time the game is in development (monthly/quarterly) and not specific game functionality - this is not as common as detailed functionality "milestone lists".

There is no industry standard for defining milestones, and such varies depending on publisher, year, or project.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003192-162) Some common milestones for a two-year development cycle are as follows:[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009244-157)

#### First playable

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: First playable")]

The *first playable* is the game version containing representative gameplay and assets,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009244-157) this is the first version with functional major gameplay elements.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003293-158) It is often based on the prototype created in pre-production.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009244–245-159) Alpha and first playable are sometimes used to refer to a single milestone, however, large projects require first playable before [feature complete](/wiki/Feature_complete "Feature complete") alpha.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003293-158) First playable occurs 12 to 18 months before code release. It is sometimes referred to as the "Pre-Alpha" stage.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161)

#### Alpha

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Alpha")]

See also: [Alpha release](/wiki/Alpha_release "Alpha release")

*Alpha* is the stage when key gameplay functionality is implemented, and assets are partially finished.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161) A game in alpha is *feature complete*, that is, the game is playable and contains all the major features.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003192-162) These features may be further revised based on testing and feedback.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161) Additional small, new features may be added, and similarly planned, but unimplemented features may be dropped.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003192-162) Programmers focus mainly on finishing the codebase, rather than implementing additions.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003294-160)

#### Code freeze

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Code freeze")]

*Code freeze* is the stage when new code is no longer added to the game and only bugs are being corrected. Code freeze occurs three to four months before code release.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161)

#### Beta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Beta")]

See also: [Beta release](/wiki/Beta_release "Beta release")

*Beta* is a feature and asset complete version of the game, when only bugs are being fixed.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003294-160)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161) This version contains no bugs that prevent the game from being shippable.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003294-160) No changes are made to the game features, assets, or code. Beta occurs two to three months before code release.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161)

#### Code release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Code release")]

*Code release* is the stage when many bugs are fixed and game is ready to be shipped or submitted for console manufacturer review. This version is tested against the QA test plan. First code release candidate is usually ready three to four weeks before code release.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2009245-161)

#### Gold master

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Gold master")]

See also: [Release to manufacturing](/wiki/Release_to_manufacturing "Release to manufacturing")

*Gold master* is the final game's build that is used as a master for the production of the game.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003295-163)

#### Release schedules and "crunch time"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Release schedules and \"crunch time\"")]

See also: [Crunch (video games)](/wiki/Crunch_(video_games) "Crunch (video games)")

In most [AAA](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)") game development, games are announced a year or more in advance and given a planned release date or approximate window so that they can promote and market the game, establish orders with retailers, and entice consumers to pre-order the game. Delaying the release of a video game can have a negative financial impact on publishers and developers, and extensive delays may lead to project cancellation and employee layoffs.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201020,48-164) To ensure a game makes a set release date, publishers and developers may require their employees to work overtime to complete the game, which is considered common in the industry.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak2010241-165) This overtime is often referred to as "[crunch time](/wiki/Crunch_(video_games) "Crunch (video games)")" or "crunch mode".[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcShaffry200917-166) In 2004 and afterwards, the culture of crunch time in the industry came under scrutiny, leading many publishers and developers to reduce the expectation on developers for overtime work and better schedule management, though crunch time still can occur.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201048,_241-167)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Post-production")]

After the game [goes gold](/wiki/Release_to_manufacturing "Release to manufacturing") and ships, some developers will give team members *comp time* (perhaps up to a week or two) to compensate for the overtime put in to complete the game, though this compensation is not standard.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Maintenance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Maintenance")]

Once a game ships, the maintenance phase for the video game begins.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201097-168)

Games developed for [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") have had almost no maintenance period in the past. The shipped game would forever house as many bugs and features as when released. This was common for consoles since all consoles had identical or nearly identical hardware; making incompatibility, the cause of many bugs, a non-issue. In this case, maintenance would only occur in the case of a [port](/wiki/Game_port "Game port"), [sequel](/wiki/Sequel "Sequel"), or [enhanced remake](/wiki/Enhanced_remake "Enhanced remake") that reuses a large portion of the engine and assets.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In recent times popularity of online console games has grown, and online capable video game consoles and online services such as [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live") for the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_(console) "Xbox (console)") have developed. Developers can maintain their software through downloadable patches. These changes would not have been possible in the past without the widespread availability of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[PC](/wiki/IBM_PC_compatible "IBM PC compatible") development is different. Game developers try to account for the majority of configurations and hardware. However, the number of possible configurations of hardware and software inevitably leads to the discovery of game-breaking circumstances that the programmers and testers did not account for.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Programmers wait for a period to get as many bug reports as possible. Once the developer thinks they've obtained enough feedback, the programmers start working on a [patch](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)"). The patch may take weeks or months to develop, but it is intended to fix most accounted bugs and problems with the game that were overlooked in past code releases, or in rare cases, fix unintended problems caused by previous patches. Occasionally a patch may include extra features or content or may even alter gameplay.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the case of a [massively multiplayer online game](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_game "Massively multiplayer online game") (MMOG), such as a [MMORPG](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_role-playing_game "Massively multiplayer online role-playing game") or [MMORTS](/wiki/MMORTS "MMORTS"), the shipment of the game is the starting phase of maintenance.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201097-168) The maintenance staff for such an online game can number in the dozens, sometimes including members of the original programming team,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] as the game world is continuously changed and iterated and new features are added. Some developers implement a public test realm or player test realm (PTR) in order to test out significant upcoming changes prior to release. These specialized servers offer similar benefits as beta testing, where players get to preview new features while the developer gathers data about bugs and game balance.[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170)

## Outsourcing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Outsourcing")]

Several development disciplines, such as audio, dialogue, or motion capture, occur for relatively short periods of time. Efficient employment of these roles requires either a large development house with multiple simultaneous title productions or outsourcing from third-party vendors.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003183-171) Employing personnel for these tasks full-time is expensive,[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003183–184-172) so a majority of developers outsource a portion of the work. Outsourcing plans are conceived during the pre-production stage; where the time and finances required for outsourced work are estimated.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003184-173)

* The music cost ranges based on the length of composition, method of performance (live or synthesized), and composer experience.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003343-174) In 2003 a minute of high-quality synthesized music cost between [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")600-1.5k.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003344-154) A title with 20 hours of gameplay and 60 minutes of music may have cost $50k-60k for its musical score.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003345-155)
* Voice acting is well-suited for outsourcing as it requires a set of specialized skills. Only large publishers employ in-house voice actors.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003353-175)
* Sound effects can also be outsourced.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003363-152)
* Programming is generally outsourced less than other disciplines, such as art or music. However, outsourcing for extra programming work or savings in salaries has become more common in recent years.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003185-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181)

### Ghost development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Ghost development")]

Outsourced work is sometimes anonymous, i.e. not credited on the final product. This might go against the wishes of the developer, or it is something they reluctantly consent to because it is the only work they can get.[[182]](#cite_note-182) See [Video game controversies § Lack of crediting](/wiki/Video_game_controversies#Lack_of_crediting "Video game controversies") for more information on this.

However, anonymity can also be agreed upon, or even desired by the outsourced party. A 2015 *[Polygon](/wiki/Polygon_(website) "Polygon (website)")* article stated that this practice is known as **ghost development**.[[183]](#cite_note-Leone_(2015)-183) Ghost developers are hired by other developers to provide assistance, by publishers to develop a title they designed, or by companies outside the gaming industry. These businesses prefer to keep this hidden from the public to protect their [brand equity](/wiki/Brand_equity "Brand equity"), not wanting consumers or investors to know that they rely on external help. Ghost development can involve (small) portions of a project, but there have been instances of entire games being outsourced without the studio being credited.[[183]](#cite_note-Leone_(2015)-183)

Ghost development has a particularly long history in the [Japanese video game industry](/wiki/Video_games_in_Japan "Video games in Japan").[[184]](#cite_note-184) Probably the best-known example is [Tose](/wiki/Tose_(company) "Tose (company)"). Founded in 1979, this 'behind-the-scenes' agent has either developed or helped develop over 2,000 games as of 2017, most of them anonymously. This includes uncredited contributions to multiple *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil "Resident Evil")*, *[Metal Gear](/wiki/Metal_Gear "Metal Gear")*, and *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest")* titles.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186) Another example is Tokyo-based Hyde, which worked on *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*, *[Persona](/wiki/Persona_(series) "Persona (series)")*, and *[Yakuza](/wiki/Yakuza_(franchise) "Yakuza (franchise)")* games.[[187]](#cite_note-187) Its president, Kenichi Yanagihara, stated that the approach stems from [Japanese culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan"), in which many people prefer not to seek the limelight.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Marketing")]

The game production has similar distribution methods to those of music and film industries.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200925-35)

The publisher's marketing team targets the game for a specific market and then advertises it.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004241-189) The team advises the developer on target demographics and market trends,[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004241-189) as well as suggests specific features.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004242-190) The game is then advertised and the game's high concept is incorporated into the promotional material, ranging from magazine ads to TV spots.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004241-189) Communication between developer and marketing is important.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004242-190)

The length and purpose of a [game demo](/wiki/First_playable_demo "First playable demo") depends on the purpose of the demo and target audience. A game's demo may range between a few seconds (such as clips or screenshots) to hours of gameplay. The demo is usually intended for journalists, buyers, trade shows, general public, or internal employees (who, for example, may need to familiarize with the game to promote it). Demos are produced with public relations, marketing and sales in mind, maximizing the presentation effectiveness.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004246-191)

### Trade show demo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Trade show demo")]

As a game nears completion, the publisher will want to showcase a [demo](/wiki/Game_demo "Game demo") of the title at trade shows. Many games have a "Trade Show demo" scheduled.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The major annual trade shows are, for example, [Electronic Entertainment Expo](/wiki/Electronic_Entertainment_Expo "Electronic Entertainment Expo") (E3) or [Penny Arcade Expo](/wiki/Penny_Arcade_Expo "Penny Arcade Expo") (PAX).[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200924–25-192) E3 is the largest show in North America.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200357-193) E3 is hosted primarily for marketing and business deals. New games and platforms are announced at E3 and it received broad press coverage.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200924-78)[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200358-194) Thousands of products are on display and press demonstration schedules are kept.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke200358-194) In the 2000s E3 became a more closed-door event and many advertisers have withdrawn, reducing E3's budget.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200924-78) PAX, created by authors of [Penny Arcade](/wiki/Penny_Arcade "Penny Arcade") blog and web-comic, is a mature and playful event with a player-centred philosophy.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200925-35)

### Localization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Localization")]

Main article: [Video game localization](/wiki/Video_game_localization "Video game localization")

A game created in one language may also be published in other countries which speak a different language. For that region, the developers may want to translate the game to make it more accessible. For example, some games created for [PlayStation Vita](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita "PlayStation Vita") were initially published in Japanese language, like *[Soul Sacrifice](/wiki/Soul_Sacrifice_(video_game) "Soul Sacrifice (video game)")*. Non-native speakers of the game's original language may have to wait for the translation of the game to their language. But most modern big-budget games take localization into account during the development process and the games are released in several different languages simultaneously.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

Localization is the process of translating the language assets in a game into other languages.[[196]](#cite_note-196) By localizing games, they increase their level of accessibility where games could help to expend the international markets effectively. Game localization is generally known as language translations yet a "full localization" of a game is a complex project. Different levels of translation range from: zero translation being that there is no translation to the product and all things are sent raw, to basic translation where only a few text and subtitles are translated or even added, and full translation where new voiceovers and game material changes are added.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are various essential elements in localizing a game including translating the language of the game to adjusting in-game assets for different cultures to reach more potential consumers in other geographies (or globalization for short). The translation seems to fall into the scope of localization, which itself constitutes a substantially broader endeavour.[[197]](#cite_note-197) These include the different levels of translation to the globalization of the game itself. However, certain developers seem to be divided on whether globalization falls under localization or not.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Moreover, to fit into the local markets, game production companies often change or redesign the graphic designs or the packaging of the game for marketing purposes. For example, the popular game *[Assassin's Creed](/wiki/Assassin%27s_Creed "Assassin's Creed")* has two different packaging designs for the European and US markets.[[198]](#cite_note-198) By localizing the graphics and packaging designs, companies might arouse better connections and attention from the consumers from various regions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Development costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Development costs")]

See also: [List of most expensive video games to develop](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_video_games_to_develop "List of most expensive video games to develop")

The costs of developing a video game varies widely depending on several factors including team size, game genre and scope, and other factors such as intellectual property licensing costs. Most video game consoles also require development licensing costs which include [game development kits](/wiki/Game_development_kit "Game development kit") for building and testing software. Game budgets also typically include costs for marketing and promotion, which can be on the same order in cost as the development budget.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

Prior to the 1990s, game development budgets, when reported, typically were on the average of US$1–5 million, with known outliers, such as the $20–25 million that Atari had paid to license the rights for *[E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial](/wiki/E.T._the_Extra-Terrestrial_(video_game) "E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (video game)")* in addition to development costs.[[200]](#cite_note-200)[[201]](#cite_note-201) The adoption of technologies such as 3D hardware rendering and CD-ROM integration by the mid-1990s, enabling games with more visual fidelity compared to prior titles, caused developers and publishers to put more money into game budgets as to flesh out narratives through [cutscenes](/wiki/Cutscene "Cutscene") and [full-motion video](/wiki/Full-motion_video "Full-motion video"), and creating the start of the [AAA video game industry](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)"). Some of the most expensive titles to develop around this time, approaching costs typical of major motion picture production budgets, included *[Final Fantasy VII](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VII "Final Fantasy VII")* in 1997 with an estimated budget of $40–45 million,[[202]](#cite_note-202) and *[Shenmue](/wiki/Shenmue_(video_game) "Shenmue (video game)")* in 1999 with an estimated budget of $47–70 million.[[203]](#cite_note-203)*Final Fantasy VII*, with its marketing budget, had a total estimated cost of $80–145 million.[[204]](#cite_note-204)

[Raph Koster](/wiki/Raph_Koster "Raph Koster"), a video game designer and economist, evaluated published development budgets (less any marketing) for over 250 games in 2017 and reported that since the mid-1990s, there has been a type of [Moore's Law](/wiki/Moore%27s_Law "Moore's Law") in game budgets, with the average budget doubling about every five years after accounting for inflation. Koster reported average budgets were around $100 million by 2017, and could reach over $200 million by the early 2020s. Koster asserts these trends are partially tied to the technological Moore's law that gave more computational power for developers to work into their games, but also related to expectations for content from players in newer games and the number of players games are expected to draw.[[205]](#cite_note-205) [Shawn Layden](/wiki/Shawn_Layden "Shawn Layden"), former CEO of [Sony Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment"), affirmed that the costs for each generation of PlayStation consoles nearly doubled, with PlayStation 4 games have average budgets of $100 million and anticipating that [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5") games could reach $200 million.[[206]](#cite_note-206)

The rising costs of budgets of AAA games in the early 2000s led publishers to become risk-averse, staying to titles that were most likely to be high-selling games to recoup their costs. As a result of this risk aversion, the selection of AAA games in the mid-2000s became rather similar, and gave the opportunity for [indie games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game") that provided more experimental and unique gameplay concepts to expand around that time.[[207]](#cite_note-207)

Costs of development for AAA games continued to rise over the next two decades; a report by the United Kingdom's [Competition and Markets Authority](/wiki/Competition_and_Markets_Authority "Competition and Markets Authority") regarding the [proposed acquisition of Activision Blizzard by Microsoft](/wiki/Proposed_acquisition_of_Activision_Blizzard_by_Microsoft "Proposed acquisition of Activision Blizzard by Microsoft") in 2023. Costs slowing increased from 1–4 million in 2000, to over $5 million in 2006, then to over $20 million by 2010, followed by $50 million to $150 million by 2018, and $200 million and up by 2023. In some cases, several AAA games exceeded $1 billion to make, split between $500-$600M to develop and a similar amount for marketing.[[208]](#cite_note-208) In court documents from regulatory review of the Activision Blizzard merger, reviewed by *[The Verge](/wiki/The_Verge "The Verge")*, the costs of Sony's first party games like *[Horizon Forbidden West](/wiki/Horizon_Forbidden_West "Horizon Forbidden West")* and *[The Last of Us Part II](/wiki/The_Last_of_Us_Part_II "The Last of Us Part II")* had exceeded $200 million.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

## Indie development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Indie development")]

Main article: [Independent video game development](/wiki/Independent_video_game_development "Independent video game development")

[Independent games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game") or *indie games*[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-210) are produced by individuals and small teams with no large-scale developer or publisher affiliations.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-210)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak2010272-211)[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBates2004252-212) Indie developers generally rely on Internet distribution schemes. Many hobbyist indie developers create [mods](/wiki/Mod_(computer_gaming) "Mod (computer gaming)") of existing games. Indie developers are credited for creative game ideas (for example, *[Darwinia](/wiki/Darwinia_(video_game) "Darwinia (video game)")*, *[Weird Worlds](/wiki/Weird_Worlds:_Return_to_Infinite_Space "Weird Worlds: Return to Infinite Space")*, *[World of Goo](/wiki/World_of_Goo "World of Goo")*). Current economic viability of indie development is questionable, however in recent years internet delivery platforms, such as, [Xbox Live Arcade](/wiki/Xbox_Live_Arcade "Xbox Live Arcade") and [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") have improved indie game success.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-210) In fact, some indie games have become very successful, such as *[Braid](/wiki/Braid_(video_game) "Braid (video game)")*,[[213]](#cite_note-BraidPop-213) *[World of Goo](/wiki/World_of_Goo "World of Goo")*,[[214]](#cite_note-GooPop-214) and *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*.[[215]](#cite_note-MinePop-215) In recent years many communities have emerged in support of indie games such as the popular indie game marketplace [Itch.io](/wiki/Itch.io "Itch.io"), indie game [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channels and a large indie community on [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)"). It is common for indie game developers to release games for free and generate revenue through other means such as [microtransactions](/wiki/Microtransactions "Microtransactions") (in-game transactions), in-game advertisements and [crowd-funding](/wiki/Crowd-funding "Crowd-funding") services like [Patreon](/wiki/Patreon "Patreon") and [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Game industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Game industry")]

Main article: [Video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry")

The video game industry (formally referred to as interactive entertainment) is the economic sector involved with the development, marketing and sale of video games. The industry sports several unique approaches.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Locales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Locales")]

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: United States")]

Further information: [List of video game companies](/wiki/List_of_video_game_companies "List of video game companies")

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), in the early history of video game development, the prominent locale for game development was the corridor from [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") to [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") in [California](/wiki/California "California").[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201050–51-216) Most new developers in the US open near such "hot beds".[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201017-16)

At present, many large publishers still operate there, such as: [Activision Blizzard](/wiki/Activision_Blizzard "Activision Blizzard"), [Capcom Entertainment](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom"), [Crystal Dynamics](/wiki/Crystal_Dynamics "Crystal Dynamics"), [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts"), [Namco Bandai Games](/wiki/Namco_Bandai_Games "Namco Bandai Games"), [Sega of America](/wiki/Sega_of_America "Sega of America"), and [Sony Computer Entertainment America](/wiki/Sony_Computer_Entertainment_America "Sony Computer Entertainment America"). However, due to the nature of game development, many publishers are present in other regions, such as [Big Fish Games](/wiki/Big_Fish_Games "Big Fish Games") (Washington), [Majesco Entertainment](/wiki/Majesco_Entertainment "Majesco Entertainment") (New Jersey), [Microsoft Corporation](/wiki/Microsoft_Corporation "Microsoft Corporation") (Washington), [Nintendo of America](/wiki/Nintendo_of_America "Nintendo of America") (Washington), and [Take-Two Interactive](/wiki/Take-Two_Interactive "Take-Two Interactive") (New York),[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201051-217)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Education")]

Many universities and design schools are offering classes specifically focused on game development.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak201019-15) Some have built strategic alliances with major game development companies.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxland2004309-218)[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreNovak2010298-219) These alliances ensure that students have access to the latest technologies and are provided the opportunity to find jobs within the gaming industry once qualified.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Many innovative ideas are presented at conferences, such as [Independent Games Festival](/wiki/Independent_Games_Festival "Independent Games Festival") (IGF) or [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference") (GDC).

Indie game development may motivate students who produce a game for their final projects or thesis and may open their own game company.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-210)

### Stability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_development&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Stability")]

Video game industry employment is fairly volatile, similar to other artistic industries including television, music, etc. Scores of game development studios crop up, work on one game, and then quickly go under.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcShaffry200919–20-220) This may be one reason why game developers tend to congregate geographically; if their current studio goes under, developers can flock to an adjacent one or start another from the ground up.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In an industry where only the top 20% of products make a profit,[[221]](#cite_note-221) it is easy to understand this fluctuation. Numerous games may start development and are cancelled, or perhaps even completed but never published. Experienced game developers may work for years and yet never ship a title: such is the nature of the business.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
